rationale: IsUnsupportedFlagError itself checks out under adversarial attack: no realistic bd failure text (bead-not-found, Dolt-lock/1105-style errors, the doctor.go schema-drift regex vocabulary) collides with 'unknown flag'+'as-of'; the errors.As chain is a clean single %w hop (RunBD -> FetchBeadAsOf -> defaultVerifyCommitted) proven with a real *exec.ExitError in tests; genuine unsupported-flag failures reliably produce that ExitError (validated live against installed bd 1.1.0). BUT empirical probing (both CLI and a direct os/exec repro of RunBD's exact code path) shows `bd show <id> --as-of <ref> --json` on bd 1.1.0 exits 0 (not non-zero) for bead-not-found and bad-ref failures -- empty stdout, diagnostic only on stderr. These genuine read failures never reach IsUnsupportedFlagError's ExitError path at all; they're only caught incidentally by parseBeadShowJSON's empty-output check, which happens to still hard-fail (safe) but discards the real bd diagnostic and is untested. This means TestDefaultVerifyCommitted_AsOfHardReadFailureNeverFallsBack's synthesized-ExitError 'lock contention' scenario may not model how bd 1.1.0's own --as-of failures actually manifest. No misclassification bug, but a real, reproducible test-coverage/diagnosability gap in exactly the area this bead is meant to make precise.
concrete_changes_required: Add a test for next.FetchBeadAsOf / defaultVerifyCommitted covering runBDFn returning (empty output, nil error) -- bd 1.1.0's actual behavior for `bd show <id> --as-of <ref> --json` on a failed committed-state read (empirically confirmed: exit 0, empty stdout, diagnostic on stderr only, for both bead-not-found and invalid-ref cases) -- and assert defaultVerifyCommitted still hard-fails and never falls back to the same-read path in this shape.

Preserve the real bd stderr diagnostic instead of the generic 'bd show %s returned empty output' when --as-of exits 0 with no usable stdout, so a recoverable complete block actually tells the operator why (e.g. 'not found: issue X as of HEAD') rather than a content-free message.

Amend TestDefaultVerifyCommitted_AsOfHardReadFailureNeverFallsBack's doc comment to note its fakeBDExitError-based 'Dolt lock contention' scenario models the cobra/ExitError failure shape, which per live testing against bd 1.1.0 is NOT how bd show --as-of's own internal errors (not-found, bad ref) actually manifest (they exit 0) -- add the exit-0/empty-output case as a companion so both real failure shapes are covered, not just the cobra one.

findings.title: No false-positive collision found in IsUnsupportedFlagError against realistic bd failure text
findings.detail: Constructed/checked bd 1.1.0's real stderr text for: bead-not-found via plain `bd show` ('Error fetching X: no issue found matching...'), bead-not-found via --as-of ('Error fetching X as of HEAD: not found: issue X as of HEAD' -- note 'as of' with a SPACE, not the hyphenated 'as-of' the checker requires), bad-ref ('...invalid ref: invalid ref format...'), and the doctor.go bdSchemaDriftRE vocabulary ('unknown column'/'unknown table'/'Error 1054', never 'unknown flag'). None contain both 'unknown flag' and 'as-of'. The adversarial 'bead title echoed into stderr' vector (suggested in the review lens) is not realizable via `bd show --as-of`: on success the bead's JSON goes to stdout only; every failure mode observed emits a bd-owned fixed template on stderr, never the bead's title/notes.

findings.title: errors.As chain to *exec.ExitError is intact through the one real wrap layer, confirmed with a genuine ExitError in tests
findings.detail: bead.RunBD/tracedOutput returns cmd.Output()'s error UNWRAPPED. next.FetchBeadAsOf wraps it exactly once via fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err). defaultVerifyCommitted calls bead.IsUnsupportedFlagError directly on that once-wrapped error, which does errors.As(err, &exitErr) -- a single Unwrap() hop, always reachable. Live check: `bd show --nonexistentflagxyz` -> exit 1, stderr 'Error: unknown flag: --nonexistentflagxyz' -- confirms the cobra/pflag unknown-flag contract (exit 1, ExitError, exact 'unknown flag: --<name>' text) generalizes soundly to an older bd that lacks --as-of. Tests use fakeBDExitError to build a REAL *exec.ExitError via cmd.Output() rather than a hand-rolled struct, which correctly exercises this exact path (not a fake that could silently diverge from reality).

findings.title: bd 1.1.0's `bd show --as-of` exits 0 on genuine read failures -- these never reach IsUnsupportedFlagError's intended detection path
findings.detail: Empirically reproduced against the installed bd 1.1.0 both via the bd CLI directly and via a standalone Go program using the exact os/exec.Cmd.Output() pattern RunBD/tracedOutput uses: `bd show somefakeid123 --as-of HEAD --json`, `bd show mindspec-zzzz9 --as-of HEAD --json` (well-formed but nonexistent ID), and `bd show mindspec-uopd --as-of HEAD~1 --json` (malformed ref) all exit 0 with EMPTY stdout and the real diagnostic on stderr only ('not found: issue X as of HEAD', 'invalid ref: invalid ref format: HEAD~1'). Because cmd.Output() returns err==nil on exit 0, next.FetchBeadAsOf's `if err != nil` branch (the only place that wraps into something IsUnsupportedFlagError can inspect) is never entered for these cases -- they fall through to parseBeadShowJSON, which returns a bare (non-%w-wrapped) 'bd show %s returned empty output' error. IsUnsupportedFlagError correctly returns false on this error (no ExitError in the chain, so no false positive), and defaultVerifyCommitted correctly hard-fails on it (safe, never proceeds on unverified close) -- so there is NO misclassification bug and NO safety violation. But: (1) the real bd stderr diagnostic is silently discarded, so operators debugging a recoverable `mindspec complete` block only see a content-free 'returned empty output' message instead of the actual cause; (2) this exact shape (err==nil, empty stdout) has zero test coverage in complete_test.go or next's tests; (3) it calls into question whether TestDefaultVerifyCommitted_AsOfHardReadFailureNeverFallsBack's assumption -- that a 'Dolt lock contention' failure would arrive as a genuine *exec.ExitError -- matches bd 1.1.0's real behavior, since every other internal (non-flag-parsing) --as-of failure observed exits 0 rather than non-zero.
